title: Global Strategy for the Diagnosis, Management, and Prevention of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (2026 Pocket Guide)
one_line_summary: The 2026 GOLD Pocket Guide provides a global strategy for the diagnosis, management, and prevention of COPD, emphasizing spirometry for diagnosis, personalized pharmacotherapy based on symptoms and exacerbation risk, and comprehensive non-pharmacological care.
key_pearls: A post-bronchodilator **FEV1/FVC < 0.7** on spirometry is **mandatory** to confirm the diagnosis of COPD in the appropriate clinical context.
Initial pharmacological treatment should be individualized based on symptom severity (mMRC/CAT) and exacerbation history, using the **GOLD ABE assessment tool**.
Systemic corticosteroids are **recommended** for up to **5 days** in patients with moderate or severe exacerbations.
Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) may improve **survival** in patients with severe resting chronic hypoxemia (**PaO2 ≤ 55 mmHg** or < **60 mmHg** with cor pulmonale or secondary polycythemia).
Pulmonary rehabilitation, including exercise training and disease-specific education, improves exercise capacity, symptoms, and quality of life across all grades of COPD severity.
An annual **low-dose CT scan (LDCT)** is **recommended** for lung cancer screening in patients with COPD and a smoking history, similar to the general population.
Following a moderate or severe exacerbation, the risk of a major cardiovascular event is **significantly** increased for up to **one year**.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- COPD is a heterogeneous lung condition characterized by chronic respiratory symptoms (dyspnea, cough, sputum production, exacerbations) due to abnormalities of the airways (bronchitis, bronchiolitis) and/or alveoli (emphysema) causing persistent, often progressive, airflow obstruction.
- COPD results from gene(G)-environment(E) interactions over the lifetime(T) of the individual (GETomics) that can damage the lungs and/or alter their normal development/aging processes.
- The main environmental exposures are tobacco smoking and inhalation of toxic particles and gases from household and outdoor air pollution.
- Other contributing factors include abnormal lung development and accelerated lung aging.
- COPD is a common, preventable, and treatable disease, but extensive under-diagnosis and misdiagnosis leads to patients receiving no or incorrect treatment.
- Precursor conditions like pre-COPD and PRISm open new windows for prevention, early diagnosis, and therapeutic intervention.

recommendations.statement: In the appropriate clinical context, the presence of non-fully reversible airflow obstruction (i.e., **FEV1/FVC < 0.7** post-bronchodilation) measured by spirometry confirms the diagnosis of COPD.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- A diagnosis of COPD should be considered in any patient with dyspnea, chronic cough or sputum production, a history of recurrent lower respiratory tract infections, and/or a history of exposure to risk factors.
- Spirometry with post-bronchodilator **FEV1/FVC < 0.7** is **mandatory** to establish the diagnosis.
- Pre-bronchodilator spirometry can be used to exclude a diagnosis of COPD.
- The goals of initial assessment are to determine the severity of airflow obstruction, assess the impact of symptoms, and assess the risk of future events (exacerbations, hospital admissions, death) to guide therapy.
- Routine follow-up of lung function, symptoms, and exacerbations is **essential** to determine when to modify management and identify complications and/or comorbidities.
- Concomitant chronic diseases (multimorbidity) occur frequently, including cardiovascular disease, skeletal muscle dysfunction, metabolic syndrome, osteoporosis, depression, anxiety, and lung cancer. These should be actively sought and treated.

recommendations.statement: Spirometry with post-bronchodilator **FEV1/FVC < 0.7** is **mandatory** to establish the diagnosis of COPD.

Concomitant chronic diseases (multimorbidity) should be actively sought and treated appropriately when present, as they influence health status, hospitalizations, and **mortality** independently of the severity of airflow obstruction.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- All individuals who smoke should be strongly encouraged and supported to quit. Nicotine replacement and pharmacotherapy reliably increase long-term smoking abstinence rates.
- There is no evidence to support the effectiveness and safety of e-cigarettes as a smoking cessation aid at present.
- People with COPD should receive all **recommended** vaccinations (COVID-19, influenza, pneumococcal, RSV, Tdap, shingles) in line with relevant local guidelines.
- Initial pharmacological treatment should be individualized and guided by the severity of symptoms, risk of exacerbations, side-effects, comorbidities, drug availability and cost, and the patient's preference and ability to use drug delivery devices.
- Inhaler technique and adherence need to be assessed regularly.
- Pulmonary rehabilitation, including exercise training combined with disease-specific education, improves exercise capacity, symptoms, and quality of life across all grades of COPD severity.
- LTOT should not be prescribed routinely for patients with stable COPD and resting or exercise-induced moderate desaturation.
- In select patients with advanced emphysema refractory to optimized medical care, surgical or bronchoscopic interventional treatments may be beneficial.
- Palliative approaches are effective in controlling symptoms in advanced COPD.

recommendations.statement: All individuals who smoke should be strongly encouraged and supported to quit.
People with COPD should receive all **recommended** vaccinations in line with the relevant local guidelines.

Initial pharmacological treatment of COPD should be individualized and guided by the severity of symptoms, risk of exacerbations, side-effects, comorbidities, drug availability and cost, and the patient's preference.
Inhaler technique and adherence need to be assessed regularly.

LTOT should not be prescribed routinely for patients with stable COPD and resting or exercise-induced moderate desaturation, but it may improve **survival** in patients with severe resting chronic hypoxemia (**PaO2 ≤ 55 mmHg** or < **60 mmHg** if there is cor pulmonale or secondary polycythemia).

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- An exacerbation of COPD is an acute event with symptoms worsening over a few days (up to 14 days) characterized by increased dyspnea and/or cough and sputum, often accompanied by tachypnea and/or tachycardia.
- Exacerbations are most frequently caused by infections (viral, bacteria) or environmental pollutants, but other conditions (pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, acute heart failure, pneumothorax) can mimic or worsen symptoms.
- Exacerbation severity is classified as mild, moderate, or severe based on clinical characteristics, according to the Rome proposal.
- Pharmacological therapy should be started as soon as possible to prevent complications.
- Maintenance therapy with LABDs should be initiated as soon as possible.
- Exacerbation recovery time varies, taking up to 4-6 weeks, with some patients failing to return to their pre-exacerbation functional state.
- Following an exacerbation, management of COPD and its comorbidities should be reviewed and appropriate measures for exacerbation prevention should be implemented.

recommendations.statement: SABAs, with or without short-acting anticholinergics, are **recommended** as the initial bronchodilators to treat moderate/severe exacerbations.
Systemic corticosteroids are **recommended** for up to **5 days** in patients with moderate/severe exacerbations.
Antibiotics are **recommended** for a total of **5 days** in patients with purulent sputum.
Methylxanthines are not **recommended** due to increased side effect profiles.
In patients with ≥ 1 moderate or severe exacerbation and elevated blood eosinophil levels, the addition of ICSs to a dual bronchodilator regimen should be considered at discharge.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- Patients with COPD often have other chronic conditions (multimorbidity) that increase the risk of poor outcomes.
- Multimorbidity is often underdiagnosed and undertreated, and should be actively searched for in each patient.
- The presence of those morbidities should not alter COPD treatment; comorbid disease should be treated as per usual standards, despite the presence of COPD.
- CVDs, particularly hypertension, ischemic heart disease, heart failure, and atrial fibrillation, are common. The risk of a major cardiovascular event is **significantly** increased during and up to one year after a moderate or severe exacerbation.
- Lung cancer is a major cause of death in patients with COPD.
- Bronchiectasis is frequently present and impacts disease progression, exacerbations, and risk of death.
- Depression/anxiety are frequent, often under-diagnosed, and associated with poor health status and increased risk of death.
- In COPD, low BMI (< **21 kg**/m2) is associated with increased risk of death. Obesity (> **30 kg**/m2) is associated with metabolic syndrome and OSA.
- GERD is associated with an increased risk of exacerbations and poor health status.
- Multiple organ loss of tissue (MOLT) manifested by osteoporosis, sarcopenia, anemia, and emphysema is associated with poor outcomes.
- When COPD is part of a multimorbidity care plan, a goal should be simplicity of treatment, minimizing polypharmacy.

recommendations.statement: Multimorbidity should be actively searched for in each patient with COPD.
The presence of morbidities should not alter COPD treatment; comorbid disease should be treated as per usual standards, despite the presence of COPD.

An annual LDCT scan is **recommended** for lung cancer screening in patients with a smoking history, similar to recommendations for the general population. Screening for lung cancer in patients with COPD not associated with tobacco smoking is not **recommended** due to lack of evidence.
When COPD is part of a multimorbidity care plan, a goal should be simplicity of treatment, minimizing polypharmacy.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technologies in COPD
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- Artificial intelligence can help in the diagnosis, assessment, clinical management, and prediction of **prognosis** of COPD.
- AI comes with risks and limitations that need careful consideration before deployment in clinical practice.
- Telehealth includes virtual, hybrid virtual and in-person care models.
- Telehealth may offer improved healthcare access, outcomes, and affordability.
- Pulmonary rehabilitation and self-management may be delivered virtually.
- Evidence is still emerging regarding the effectiveness of virtual compared to in-person delivery.

bedside_protocol.title: Identify Dates, Diagnosis, and Follow-up Type
bedside_protocol.action: Identify dates, Dx and whether this follow-up is being done in-person, by phone or remotely.

bedside_protocol.title: Assess Baseline Symptoms
bedside_protocol.action: Go over the patient symptoms and whether there have been changes in dyspnea, cough, sputum volume and color (from least to most purulent: mucus; mucopurulent; purulent). Identify maintenance pharmacological and non-pharmacological treatment and whether the patient is observing treatment as prescribed.

bedside_protocol.title: Assess Airborne Virus Status
bedside_protocol.action: Assess whether the patient has any symptoms of airborne virus infection and would need to be tested. Have at hand local numbers where the patient can be referred to for testing and treatment. If the patient has already been tested identify when the results will be obtained, or whether the result was positive or negative. Verify patient is practicing precautions (face masks, hand washing, social distancing, or shielding if necessary).

bedside_protocol.title: Review Action Plan
bedside_protocol.action: Describe if the patient already has a written action plan. Describe if the education for this action plan has already been done. Describe if the written action plan includes a prescription to be self-administered at home or whether the patient need to call his contact person / physician to obtain the prescription. Describe when it was used the last time and if used appropriately.

bedside_protocol.title: Review Recent Admissions and ED Visits
bedside_protocol.action: Write down recent admissions and ED visits, dates and where they took place.

bedside_protocol.title: Assess COPD Self-management Behaviors
bedside_protocol.action: Go over each of the self-management behaviors described in the list. You should cover what is pertinent to the patient treatable traits (dyspnea and/or exacerbation). Describe whether the patient has integrated these strategies in their daily life (yes), not at all (e.g., it has not been discussed or not applicable), and whether the patient is unsure 'cannot tell'.

bedside_protocol.title: Identify Main Issues
bedside_protocol.action: Identify with the patient the main issues of the call. Up to a maximum of 3 items that can be covered for the duration of the call. Avoid covering too many issues in one visit.

bedside_protocol.title: Summarize, Intervene, and Plan
bedside_protocol.action: Finalize by describing the interventions done during the remote visit, the ones to be put in place, and agreed by the patient, the plan, including whether the patient needs to be referred to other services, healthcare professionals, etc. and when the next follow-up will take place (describe whether will it be in-person or remote).

strengths_limitations:
- Strengths: Provides a concise, globally applicable summary of the comprehensive GOLD 2026 report; includes a practical bedside follow-up checklist; emphasizes new concepts like GETomics, pre-COPD, and PRISm.
- Limitations: As a pocket guide, it lacks detailed evidence levels and specific citations found in the full report; recommendations are generalized and may need adaptation to local contexts and drug availability.

drugs_doses.indication: Smoking cessation aid for individuals with COPD who smoke
Initial bronchodilator for moderate/severe COPD exacerbations
Initial bronchodilator for moderate/severe COPD exacerbations (with or without SABAs)

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; duration up to 5 days

drugs_doses.indication: Add-on to dual bronchodilator regimen at discharge for patients with ≥1 moderate/severe exacerbation and elevated blood eosinophils
Maintenance therapy for COPD; should be initiated as soon as possible after an exacerbation
